text: exchangeable dyad, interdep generates arbitrary member-difference column, named .i_diff_*, +1 one member -1 . exchangeable residual structure represented two separate random-effects terms: shared dyad random intercept random coefficient difference column (del Rosario West 2025). random effects denoted umean,ju_{\mathrm{mean},j} udiff,ju_{\mathrm{diff},j}, two members receive r1j=umean,j+udiff,j,r2j=umean,j−udiff,j. r_{1j} = u_{\mathrm{mean},j} + u_{\mathrm{diff},j}, \qquad r_{2j} = u_{\mathrm{mean},j} - u_{\mathrm{diff},j}. shared difference effects fitted separate random-effects terms, uncorrelated. Writing variances σmean2\sigma_{\mathrm{mean}}^2 σdiff2\sigma_{\mathrm{diff}}^2 gives (umean,judiff,j)∼𝒩[(00),(σmean200σdiff2)]. \begin{pmatrix} u_{\mathrm{mean},j} \\ u_{\mathrm{diff},j} \end{pmatrix} \sim \mathcal{N} \left[ \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}, \begin{pmatrix} \sigma_{\mathrm{mean}}^2 & 0 \\ 0 & \sigma_{\mathrm{diff}}^2 \end{pmatrix} \right]. implied member-level residual covariance matrix therefore 𝚺exch=(σmean2+σdiff2σmean2−σdiff2σmean2−σdiff2σmean2+σdiff2). \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{\mathrm{exch}} = \begin{pmatrix} \sigma_{\mathrm{mean}}^2 + \sigma_{\mathrm{diff}}^2 & \sigma_{\mathrm{mean}}^2 - \sigma_{\mathrm{diff}}^2 \\ \sigma_{\mathrm{mean}}^2 - \sigma_{\mathrm{diff}}^2 & \sigma_{\mathrm{mean}}^2 + \sigma_{\mathrm{diff}}^2 \end{pmatrix}. Thus, members residual variance v=σmean2+σdiff2v = \sigma_{\mathrm{mean}}^2 + \sigma_{\mathrm{diff}}^2, covariance c=σmean2−σdiff2c = \sigma_{\mathrm{mean}}^2 - \sigma_{\mathrm{diff}}^2, residual correlation c/vc/v. Conversely, shared difference variances can recovered member-level variance covariance σmean2=v+c2,σdiff2=v−c2. \sigma_{\mathrm{mean}}^2 = \frac{v + c}{2}, \qquad \sigma_{\mathrm{diff}}^2 = \frac{v - c}{2}. example, random-intercept variance 1.2 difference variance 0.3 imply member variance 1.5 covariance partners 0.9. code: apim_exchangeable_data <- prepare_interdep_data(   example_dyadic_crosssectional,   group = coupleID,   member = personID,   role = gender,   predictors = communication,   set_exchangeable_compositions = "female-male" ) apim_exchangeable_model <- glmmTMB::glmmTMB(   satisfaction ~ 0 +     .i_is_female_x_male +     .i_communication_actor +     .i_communication_partner +     (0 + .i_is_female_x_male | coupleID) +     (0 + .i_diff_female_x_male_arbitrary | coupleID),   dispformula = ~ 0,   family = gaussian(),   data = apim_exchangeable_data ) compare_interdep_models(   restricted = apim_exchangeable_model,   full = apim_distinguishable_model ) #> Likelihood-ratio test for nested models fitted to equivalent interdep data #> Mathematical nesting is assumed and cannot be verified from the data alone. #>  #>                            Df    AIC    BIC  logLik deviance  Chisq Chi Df #> apim_exchangeable_model     5 603.98 619.84 -296.99   593.98               #> apim_distinguishable_model  9 589.49 618.03 -285.75   571.49 22.492      4 #>                            Pr(>Chisq)     #> apim_exchangeable_model                   #> apim_distinguishable_model  0.0001599 *** #> --- #> Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1 #>  #> Under the assumed nesting and chi-squared reference distribution, the test provides evidence that `apim_exchangeable_model` fits the data worse than `apim_distinguishable_model` (likelihood-ratio test: χ²(4) = 22.49, p < .001).

title: Dyad-Individual Model
text: DIM APIM models already use sum--difference random-effects parameterization (del Rosario West 2025). See exchangeable residual-structure section APIM vignette derivation back-transformation member-level covariance matrix.

title: Dyad-Individual Model
text: longitudinal DIM, predictors decomposed within-person -person components dyadic decomposition (Bolger Laurenceau 2013; Gistelinck Loeys 2020). default "auto" selects "time_2l" time predictors supplied. also retains raw dyad-occasion means within-dyad deviations. decomposed columns used : cwp dyad mean captures shared occasion-specific shift two members’ usual levels (shared occasion-level variation). cwp within-dyad deviation captures member usual level occasion. cbp dyad mean captures dyad’s shared usual level relative sample’s grand mean (stable -dyad differences). cbp within-dyad deviation captures member’s stable difference dyad’s usual level. cbp terms use member’s mean across observed occasions estimate member’s longer-run usual level. occasions (small TT), especially predictor low stability time, person means can unreliable. associated -person estimates can therefore biased imprecise, interpreted cautiously (Gottfredson 2019). Use temporal_predictor_decomposition = "none" construct raw dyad-occasion mean within-dyad deviation. example estimates -day associations support closeness includes diaryday adjust linear trend across study.

what: Interpretation of concurrent ILD DIM coefficients
title: Dyad-Individual Model
text: interpretation applies longitudinally: dyad-mean effects describe expected outcomes individual members expected couple-average outcomes, whereas within-dyad effects describe member deviations expected differences partners. cbp dyad-mean estimate (1.51) means , comparing couples whose average usual support differs one point holding stable difference partners constant, expected couple-average closeness 1.51 points higher higher-support couple. Equivalently, member expected report 1.51 points higher closeness. cwp dyad-mean estimate (0.49) means members one point respective usual support levels, member’s expected closeness 0.49 points higher usual levels, holding difference momentary deviations constant. Equivalently, expected couple-average closeness 0.49 points higher occasion. cbp within-dyad estimate (0.78) means partners differ one point usual support levels, expected differ 0.78 points closeness, holding couple’s average usual support predictors constant. member terms, suppose one member 0.5 points couple’s average usual support 0.5 points . expected closeness 0.39 points couple’s predicted mean, respectively. cwp within-dyad estimate (0.06) means one partner’s momentary deviation usual support one point higher partner’s deviation, expected differ 0.06 points closeness, holding occasion-specific dyad mean predictors constant. member terms, suppose momentary deviations 0.5 points occasion-specific dyad mean. expected closeness 0.03 points couple’s predicted mean, respectively.

what: Equivalence of APIM and DIM in ILD
title: Dyad-Individual Model
text: equivalent APIM uses actor partner effects levels: two ILD models identical fit statistics: equivalence holds separately within-person (cwp) -person (cbp) predictor components. within-person component: bcwp,mean=bcwp,actor+bcwp,partner b_{\mathrm{cwp,mean}} = b_{\mathrm{cwp,actor}} + b_{\mathrm{cwp,partner}} bcwp,diff=bcwp,actor−bcwp,partner b_{\mathrm{cwp,diff}} = b_{\mathrm{cwp,actor}} - b_{\mathrm{cwp,partner}} -person component: bcbp,mean=bcbp,actor+bcbp,partner b_{\mathrm{cbp,mean}} = b_{\mathrm{cbp,actor}} + b_{\mathrm{cbp,partner}} bcbp,diff=bcbp,actor−bcbp,partner b_{\mathrm{cbp,diff}} = b_{\mathrm{cbp,actor}} - b_{\mathrm{cbp,partner}} also means APIM parameterization can used one level DIM parameterization . example: mixed parameterization still estimates model:
code: apim_ILD <- glmmTMB::glmmTMB(   closeness ~     1 +      diaryday +      # Within-person APIM     .i_provided_support_cwp_actor +     .i_provided_support_cwp_partner +      # Between-person APIM     .i_provided_support_cbp_actor +     .i_provided_support_cbp_partner +      # Stable exchangeable dyad-level covariance     (1 | coupleID)  + (0 + .i_diff_assumed_exchangeable_arbitrary | coupleID) +      # Residual (same-day) exchangeable dyad-level covariance     (1 | coupleID:diaryday) +     (0 + .i_diff_assumed_exchangeable_arbitrary | coupleID:diaryday)    , dispformula = ~ 0   , family = gaussian()   , data = ild_exchangeable_data ) data.frame(   model = c("DIM", "APIM"),   AIC = c(AIC(dim_ILD), AIC(apim_ILD)),   BIC = c(BIC(dim_ILD), BIC(apim_ILD)),   logLik = c(as.numeric(logLik(dim_ILD)), as.numeric(logLik(apim_ILD))) ) #>   model      AIC      BIC    logLik #> 1   DIM 2977.225 3026.637 -1478.613 #> 2  APIM 2977.225 3026.637 -1478.613 apim_dim_ILD <- glmmTMB::glmmTMB(   closeness ~     1 +      diaryday +      # Within-person APIM     .i_provided_support_cwp_actor +     .i_provided_support_cwp_partner +      # Between-person DIM     .i_provided_support_cbp_dyad_mean +     .i_provided_support_cbp_within_dyad_dev +      # Stable exchangeable dyad-level covariance     (1 | coupleID)  + (0 + .i_diff_assumed_exchangeable_arbitrary | coupleID) +      # Same-day exchangeable dyad-level covariance     (1 | coupleID:diaryday) +     (0 + .i_diff_assumed_exchangeable_arbitrary | coupleID:diaryday)    , dispformula = ~ 0   , family = gaussian()   , data = ild_exchangeable_data ) data.frame(   model = c("DIM within / DIM between", "APIM within / APIM between", "APIM within / DIM between"),   AIC = c(AIC(dim_ILD), AIC(apim_ILD), AIC(apim_dim_ILD)),   BIC = c(BIC(dim_ILD), BIC(apim_ILD), BIC(apim_dim_ILD)),   logLik = c(     as.numeric(logLik(dim_ILD)),     as.numeric(logLik(apim_ILD)),     as.numeric(logLik(apim_dim_ILD))   ) ) #>                        model      AIC      BIC    logLik #> 1   DIM within / DIM between 2977.225 3026.637 -1478.613 #> 2 APIM within / APIM between 2977.225 3026.637 -1478.613 #> 3  APIM within / DIM between 2977.225 3026.637 -1478.613

title: Dyad-Individual Model
text: Random slopes can included DIM adding corresponding within-person effects stable dyad-level random-effect blocks: following syntax illustrates full random-slope specification. fitted example data support complex random-effects structure. first stable dyad-level random effects block contains shared DIM intercept, dyad-mean slope, within-dyad-deviation slope. second block contains member-difference counterparts, included .i_diff_* interactions. uncorrelated blocks allow two members different random slopes preserving exchangeability.
code: dim_ILD_random <- glmmTMB::glmmTMB(   closeness ~     1 +      diaryday +      # Within-person DIM     .i_provided_support_cwp_dyad_mean +     .i_provided_support_cwp_within_dyad_dev +      # Between-person DIM     .i_provided_support_cbp_dyad_mean +     .i_provided_support_cbp_within_dyad_dev +      # Stable dyad-level covariance with within-person random slopes     (1 +        .i_provided_support_cwp_dyad_mean +        .i_provided_support_cwp_within_dyad_dev      | coupleID)  +     (0 +        .i_diff_assumed_exchangeable_arbitrary +        .i_diff_assumed_exchangeable_arbitrary:.i_provided_support_cwp_dyad_mean +        .i_diff_assumed_exchangeable_arbitrary:.i_provided_support_cwp_within_dyad_dev      | coupleID) +      # Same-day exchangeable dyad-level covariance     (1 | coupleID:diaryday) +     (0 + .i_diff_assumed_exchangeable_arbitrary | coupleID:diaryday)    , dispformula = ~ 0   , family = gaussian()   , data = ild_exchangeable_data )

what: Transforming DIM random slopes to APIM slopes
title: Dyad-Individual Model
text: Applying transformation random-slope coefficients proceeds two steps. First, transform DIM dyad-mean within-dyad-deviation random slopes APIM actor partner random slopes. Random effects use uu, subscripts write actor, partner, mean, diff. shared block, uactor,j=umean,j+udiff,j2,upartner,j=umean,j−udiff,j2, u_{\mathrm{actor},j} = \frac{u_{\mathrm{mean},j} + u_{\mathrm{diff},j}}{2}, \qquad u_{\mathrm{partner},j} = \frac{u_{\mathrm{mean},j} - u_{\mathrm{diff},j}}{2}, .i_diff_* block, marked tilde, ũactor,j=ũmean,j+ũdiff,j2,ũpartner,j=ũmean,j−ũdiff,j2. \widetilde{u}_{\mathrm{actor},j} = \frac{\widetilde{u}_{\mathrm{mean},j} + \widetilde{u}_{\mathrm{diff},j}}{2}, \qquad \widetilde{u}_{\mathrm{partner},j} = \frac{\widetilde{u}_{\mathrm{mean},j} - \widetilde{u}_{\mathrm{diff},j}}{2}. shared .i_diff_* random intercepts remain unchanged. now shared .i_diff_* actor partner effects back-transformed complete readily interpretable member-specific actor-partner covariance matrix. described exchangeable random-slope back-transformation APIM vignette.

title: Dyad-Individual Model
text: ILD models model residual serial dependence. One way model dynamics account temporal dependency include lagged outcomes predictors. Note: Dynamic models, especially small time series, subject bias. , choice raw within-person-centered outcome lags, addressed APIM vignette’s discussion dynamic models. Brief example obtain lagged raw within-person centered versions outcome: Lags matched exactly diaryday - 1, omitted diary days bridged. raw lagged outcome scores can included model follows: lagged dyad-mean coefficient describes one-point higher shared prior outcome level relates members’ current outcomes. lagged within-dyad-deviation coefficient describes one-point prior difference partners relates current expected difference. predictor effects model conditional members’ prior outcomes. title: Dyadic Score Model (DSM)
text: outcomes given predictors, long-format model estimates paths conventional score-based DSM (Iida et al. 2018). conventional score-based representation, predictors decomposed way: Xmean=Xfemale+Xmale2−μX,Xdiff=Xfemale−Xmale, X_{\mathrm{mean}} = \frac{X_{\mathrm{female}} + X_{\mathrm{male}}}{2} - \mu_X, \qquad X_{\mathrm{diff}} = X_{\mathrm{female}} - X_{\mathrm{male}}, μX\mu_X sample grand mean dyad-level predictor means. outcomes also decomposed: Ymean=Yfemale+Ymale2,Ydiff=Yfemale−Ymale. Y_{\mathrm{mean}} = \frac{Y_{\mathrm{female}} + Y_{\mathrm{male}}}{2}, \qquad Y_{\mathrm{diff}} = Y_{\mathrm{female}} - Y_{\mathrm{male}}. long-format model fitted create YmeanY_{\mathrm{mean}} YdiffY_{\mathrm{diff}} observed variables. Instead, uses member-level outcome directly. complete outcome pairs, equivalent parameterization conditional outcome regressions. Consider conceptual SEM formulas: Ymean̂=a10+a11Xmean+a12Xdiff, \widehat{Y_{\mathrm{mean}}} = a_{10} + a_{11}X_{\mathrm{mean}} + a_{12}X_{\mathrm{diff}}, Ydiff̂=a20+a21Xmean+a22Xdiff. \widehat{Y_{\mathrm{diff}}} = a_{20} + a_{21}X_{\mathrm{mean}} + a_{22}X_{\mathrm{diff}}. fitted paths example : Fitted cross-sectional DSM example data. Edge intercept labels show estimated DSM coefficients. fixed effects MLM model map directly paths : Thus, example, a12a_{12} change dyad-average satisfaction associated one-unit larger female-minus-male communication difference, holding communication level constant. contrast, a22a_{22} change female-minus-male satisfaction difference associated one-unit larger communication difference, holding communication level constant. a12a_{12} a21a_{21} coefficients DSM cross-paths. omitted reduced DSM needed full model. random intercept variance unexplained variation outcome level, random role-contrast slope variance unexplained variation full directional outcome difference. covariance indicates whether unexplained outcome level unexplained outcome difference associated.

code: cross_dsm_data_inverted <- prepare_interdep_data(   example_dyadic_crosssectional,   group = coupleID,   member = personID,   role = gender,   predictors = communication,   # Request APIM columns too for comparison below.   model_type = c("dsm", "apim"),   dsm_role_order = c("male", "female") ) dsm_model_inverted <- glmmTMB::glmmTMB(   satisfaction ~     .i_communication_dyad_mean_gmc +     .i_communication_within_dyad_diff +     .i_dsm_role_contrast +     .i_communication_dyad_mean_gmc:.i_dsm_role_contrast +     .i_communication_within_dyad_diff:.i_dsm_role_contrast +     (1 + .i_dsm_role_contrast | coupleID),   dispformula = ~ 0,   family = gaussian(),   data = cross_dsm_data_inverted )  female_minus_male <- glmmTMB::fixef(dsm_model)$cond male_minus_female <- glmmTMB::fixef(dsm_model_inverted)$cond  knitr::kable(   data.frame(     `model term` = names(female_minus_male),     `female - male` = unname(female_minus_male),     `male - female` = unname(male_minus_female),     check.names = FALSE   ),   digits = 3,   align = c("l", "r", "r") )

title: Dyadic Score Model (DSM)
text: Let bactor,femaleb_{\mathrm{actor},\mathrm{female}} bactor,maleb_{\mathrm{actor},\mathrm{male}} denote actor effects female male outcomes. corresponding partner effects bpartner,femaleb_{\mathrm{partner},\mathrm{female}} bpartner,maleb_{\mathrm{partner},\mathrm{male}}, APIM intercepts b0,femaleb_{0,\mathrm{female}} b0,maleb_{0,\mathrm{male}}. Fixed APIM coefficients use bb write effect outcome role. numbered paths a10a_{10} a22a_{22} retain published DSM notation. slope transformation can understood two steps. First, outcome role r∈{female,male}r \\{\mathrm{female},\mathrm{male}\}, form actor-plus-partner actor-minus-partner combinations: bsum,r=bactor,r+bpartner,r,bdifference,r=bactor,r−bpartner,r. \begin{aligned} b_{\mathrm{sum},r} &= b_{\mathrm{actor},r} + b_{\mathrm{partner},r}, \\ b_{\mathrm{difference},r} &= b_{\mathrm{actor},r} - b_{\mathrm{partner},r}. \end{aligned} DSM slopes follow combining female- male-outcome effects: a11=bsum,female+bsum,male2,a21=bsum,female−bsum,male,a12=bdifference,female−bdifference,male4,a22=bdifference,female+bdifference,male2. \begin{aligned} a_{11} &= \frac{b_{\mathrm{sum},\mathrm{female}}         + b_{\mathrm{sum},\mathrm{male}}}{2}, & a_{21} &= b_{\mathrm{sum},\mathrm{female}}    - b_{\mathrm{sum},\mathrm{male}}, \\ a_{12} &= \frac{b_{\mathrm{difference},\mathrm{female}}         - b_{\mathrm{difference},\mathrm{male}}}{4}, & a_{22} &= \frac{b_{\mathrm{difference},\mathrm{female}}         + b_{\mathrm{difference},\mathrm{male}}}{2}. \end{aligned} APIM predictors retain original scale, whereas DSM predictor level grand-mean centered. Keeping raw APIM predictors original scale preserves reference values; centering difference handled explicitly intercept transformation. μX\mu_X grand mean subtracted DSM predictor level, intercepts transform a10=b0,female+b0,male2+μXa11,a20=b0,female−b0,male+μXa21. a_{10} = \frac{b_{0,\mathrm{female}} + b_{0,\mathrm{male}}}{2} + \mu_X a_{11}, \qquad a_{20} = b_{0,\mathrm{female}} - b_{0,\mathrm{male}} + \mu_X a_{21}. reverse slope transformation, first recover role-specific actor-plus-partner actor-minus-partner combinations: bsum,female=a11+a212,bsum,male=a11−a212,bdifference,female=a22+2a12,bdifference,male=a22−2a12. \begin{aligned} b_{\mathrm{sum},\mathrm{female}} &= a_{11} + \frac{a_{21}}{2}, & b_{\mathrm{sum},\mathrm{male}} &= a_{11} - \frac{a_{21}}{2}, \\ b_{\mathrm{difference},\mathrm{female}} &= a_{22} + 2a_{12}, & b_{\mathrm{difference},\mathrm{male}} &= a_{22} - 2a_{12}. \end{aligned} , outcome role rr, bactor,r=bsum,r+bdifference,r2,bpartner,r=bsum,r−bdifference,r2. b_{\mathrm{actor},r} = \frac{b_{\mathrm{sum},r} + b_{\mathrm{difference},r}}{2}, \qquad b_{\mathrm{partner},r} = \frac{b_{\mathrm{sum},r} - b_{\mathrm{difference},r}}{2}. intercepts transform back b0,female=a10+a202−μX(a11+a212), b_{0,\mathrm{female}} = a_{10} + \frac{a_{20}}{2} - \mu_X\left(a_{11} + \frac{a_{21}}{2}\right), b0,male=a10−a202−μX(a11−a212). b_{0,\mathrm{male}} = a_{10} - \frac{a_{20}}{2} - \mu_X\left(a_{11} - \frac{a_{21}}{2}\right). following comparison applies APIM--DSM transformation six fixed effects: APIM--DSM fixed-effect transformation (centering constant = 5.148).

title: Getting Started
text: interdep helps researchers prepare cross-sectional intensive longitudinal dyadic data (generalized) multilevel models. automatically creates model-ready columns dyadic multilevel model parameterizations Actor-Partner Interdependence Models (APIM), Dyad-Individual Models (DIM), Dyadic Score Models (DSM). DIM currently supports one exchangeable dyad composition, whereas DSM supports one distinguishable dyad composition. vignette focuses automatic data preparation multilevel models (MLMs). comparison MLM structural equation modeling (SEM) approaches dyadic data, see Ledermann Kenny (2017). guidance examples use prepared data estimate cross-sectional intensive longitudinal APIMs exchangeable distinguishable dyads, test distinguishability, see Actor-Partner Interdependence Model vignette. models combine distinguishable exchangeable dyad compositions, see Mixed-Composition APIM vignette. guidance use Dyad-Individual Model (DIM) parameterization, including dyad-mean within-dyad-deviation predictors equivalence APIM effects (via interactive example) exchangeable dyads, see Dyad-Individual Model vignette. dyadic score parameterization relationship APIM distinguishable dyads, see Dyadic Score Model vignette. -depth tutorial covering data preparation model fitting, also additional steps like diagnostics assumption checks, see Distinguishable Exchangeable Dyads: Bayesian Multilevel Modelling.

title: APIMs with Mixed Dyad Compositions

text: regular APIM vignette introduces compare_interdep_models() comparing distinguishable APIM exchangeable restriction. , use approach mixed-composition constraint. treat female-male dyads exchangeable pool male-male dyads, modeling female-female dyads separately. applied analysis, constraints require theoretical empirical justification. , constrained model fits substantially worse full model. code: mixed_cross_data_constrained <- prepare_interdep_data(   example_dyadic_crosssectional_mixed,   group = coupleID,   member = personID,   role = gender,   set_exchangeable_compositions = "male-female",   pool_compositions = list(     "non_female_x_female" = c("male-male", "female-male")   ),   seed = 123 )  mixed_cross_gaussian_model_constrained <- glmmTMB::glmmTMB(   satisfaction ~ 0 +     .i_is_female_x_female +     .i_is_non_female_x_female +      # exchangeable female-female residual covariance via sum-diff     (0 + .i_is_female_x_female | coupleID) +     (0 + .i_diff_female_x_female_arbitrary | coupleID) +      # pooled female-male and male-male residual covariance via sum-diff     (0 + .i_is_non_female_x_female | coupleID) +     (0 + .i_diff_non_female_x_female_arbitrary | coupleID)    , dispformula = ~ 0   , family = gaussian()   , data = mixed_cross_data_constrained )  compare_interdep_models(   restricted = mixed_cross_gaussian_model_constrained,   full = mixed_cross_gaussian_model ) #> Likelihood-ratio test for nested models fitted to equivalent interdep data #> Mathematical nesting is assumed and cannot be verified from the data alone. #>  #>                                        Df    AIC    BIC   logLik deviance #> mixed_cross_gaussian_model_constrained  6 2087.8 2114.6 -1037.90   2075.8 #> mixed_cross_gaussian_model             11 2010.0 2059.0  -993.97   1988.0 #>                                         Chisq Chi Df Pr(>Chisq)     #> mixed_cross_gaussian_model_constrained                              #> mixed_cross_gaussian_model             87.856      5  < 2.2e-16 *** #> --- #> Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1 #>  #> Under the assumed nesting and chi-squared reference distribution, the test provides evidence that `mixed_cross_gaussian_model_constrained` fits the data worse than `mixed_cross_gaussian_model` (likelihood-ratio test: χ²(5) = 87.86, p < .001).

Gender-specific intercepts     0 + .i_is_female_x_male_female + .i_is_female_x_male_male +      # Gender-specific actor effects     .i_communication_actor:.i_is_female_x_male_female +     .i_communication_actor:.i_is_female_x_male_male +      # Gender-specific partner effects     .i_communication_partner:.i_is_female_x_male_female +     .i_communication_partner:.i_is_female_x_male_male +      # Dyad-level random effects represent the two members'     # residual covariance structure     (0 + .i_is_female_x_male_female + .i_is_female_x_male_male | coupleID),    # With the residual covariance represented by the dyad-level   # random effects above, the Gaussian residual dispersion is fixed near zero.   dispformula = ~ 0,   family = gaussian(),   data = prepared_data )

title: Compare nested models fitted to equivalent interdep data — compare_interdep_models
code: if (requireNamespace("glmmTMB", quietly = TRUE)) {   restricted_data <- prepare_interdep_data(     example_dyadic_crosssectional,     group = coupleID,     member = personID,     role = gender   )   full_data <- restricted_data    restricted_model <- glmmTMB::glmmTMB(     satisfaction ~ 1 + (1 | coupleID),     data = restricted_data   )   full_model <- glmmTMB::glmmTMB(     satisfaction ~ gender + (1 | coupleID),     data = full_data   )    compare_interdep_models(     full = full_model,     restricted = restricted_model   ) } #> Likelihood-ratio test for nested models fitted to equivalent interdep data #> Mathematical nesting is assumed and cannot be verified from the data alone. #>  #>                  Df    AIC    BIC  logLik deviance  Chisq Chi Df Pr(>Chisq)    #> restricted_model  3 917.29 926.98 -455.64   911.29                             #> full_model        4 908.52 921.45 -450.26   900.52 10.765      1   0.001034 ** #> --- #> Signif. codes:  0 ‘***’ 0.001 ‘**’ 0.01 ‘*’ 0.05 ‘.’ 0.1 ‘ ’ 1 #>  #> Under the assumed nesting and chi-squared reference distribution, the test provides evidence that `restricted_model` fits the data worse than `full_model` (likelihood-ratio test: χ²(1) = 10.77, p = 0.001).

title: Prepare dyadic data for multilevel models — prepare_interdep_data
text: data data frame tibble. Data must long format. cross-sectional dyadic data, observed member dyad one row. intensive longitudinal dyadic data, observed member dyad one row per observed time point. group Column identifying dyad. member Column identifying person member within dyad. role Optional column identifying stable member role, gender. Values must stable within group x member must contain _x_. Missing role information controlled missing_role. role supplied, dyads treated type exchangeable dyads. time Optional column identifying time measurement order repeated measures. predictors Optional variables use temporal predictor decomposition model-ready predictor construction. lag_predictors Optional subset predictors lag-1 model-ready columns created. Requires time finite, integer-valued numeric measurement index. Lagging respects dyad member structure, matches observations exactly time - 1, bridge missing occasions. raw within-person predictors lagged. Stable -person versions . model_type Model-ready column families construct. Can contain one "apim", "dim", "dsm". "apim" creates actor partner predictors. "dim" creates dyad-mean within-dyad-deviation predictors. "dsm" creates dyadic-score model predictor columns. "none" skips model-specific predictor construction validation, composition inference, optional temporal predictor decomposition, must used alone. "dim" "dsm" must requested separate calls. dsm_role_order model_type = "dsm", character vector giving two distinguishable roles order used directional differences. example, c("female", "male") defines predictor differences female minus male assigns DSM role contrast +0.5 female partners -0.5 male partners. Required DSM columns requested must NULL otherwise. temporal_predictor_decomposition Temporal decomposition strategy predictors. "none" leaves predictors undecomposed model-specific columns constructed. "time_2l" indicates two-level temporal predictor decomposition within-person -person components. "auto" resolves "time_2l" time predictors supplied, "none" otherwise. "time_2l" retains raw model-ready predictors addition within-person -person components. longitudinal DIM DSM construction, raw within-person dyadic scores computed within dyad occasion, -person scores computed within dyads. Raw DIM DSM dyad means grand-mean centered. include raw, within-person, -person versions contemporaneous predictor one model linearly dependent. set_exchangeable_compositions Optionally specify dyad compositions treat exchangeable, roles otherwise imply distinguishability. Requires role. Compositions already exchangeable listed. composition must supplied one string, using _x_, -, _, whitespace ( ) two role labels, example "female_x_male", "female-male", "female_male", "female male", arbitrary order. set multiple compositions, use character vector strings. include_compositions Optional observed dyad compositions keep exchangeability overrides pooling. Requires role. Composition references use format set_exchangeable_compositions. pool_compositions Optionally pool exchangeable dyad compositions shared final composition label. Must named list name final composition label value character vector composition references, example list(same_sex_couples = c("female-female", "male-male")). exchangeable compositions can pooled. pool must contain least two distinct observed compositions composition references resolved. incomplete_dyads handle dyads contain exactly two unique members anywhere data. "error" stops error "drop" removes entire dyad. missing_role handle missing values role column. "error" stops error, "drop" removes dyads incomplete role information. Ignored role column supplied. seed Optional seed random .i_diff_* sign assignment exchangeable dyads. NULL, current R session's RNG state used.

title: Validate dyadic input data — validate_interdep_data
text: data long-format data frame tibble. group Column identifying dyad. member Column identifying two people members within dyad, person ID. role Optional column identifying role can used distinguish partners within dyad, gender. role supplied, dyads data treated exchangeable. time Optional column identifying time measurement order. predictors Optional variables select store metadata temporal predictor decomposition model-helper functions. lag_predictors Optional subset predictors lag-1 model-ready columns created. Requires finite, integer-valued numeric time variable. model_type Requested model-ready column families. Can contain one "apim", "dim", "dsm". "none" indicates model-specific predictor construction must used alone. dsm_role_order model_type = "dsm", character vector giving two distinguishable roles order used directional differences. Required DSM columns requested must NULL otherwise. temporal_predictor_decomposition Requested temporal predictor decomposition strategy predictors. "none" leaves predictors undecomposed model-specific columns constructed. "time_2l" indicates two-level temporal predictor decomposition within-person -person components. "auto" resolves "time_2l" time predictors supplied, "none" otherwise. Model-specific helpers may apply additional conventions, grand-mean centering raw DIM DSM dyad means. incomplete_dyads handle dyads contain exactly two unique members anywhere data. "error" stops error "drop" removes entire dyad. missing_role handle missing values role column. "error" stops error, "drop" removes dyads incomplete role information. Ignored role column supplied.
